metadata.title: What A Service — Turn Your Freshworks Investment into Competitive Advantage
metadata.description: Freshworks consulting specialist for mid-market and enterprise. Freshservice (ITSM, ESM, ITAM), Freshdesk (omnichannel CX), Freddy AI. ITIL 4, PRINCE2, DORA. Deployment, migration, optimization. Results in 90 days.

hero.tagline: Freshworks Specialist — ITSM · CX · ESM · Agentic AI
hero.headline: Turn Your Freshworks Investment into Competitive Advantage
hero.subheadline: Your IT team spends 70% of its time on repetitive requests. Your tools are there but underutilized. We deploy platform expertise and results-driven delivery to unlock efficiency your organization didn’t know it had.
hero.imageAlt: Consulting team collaborating on an IT transformation project

problems.headline: Freshworks is easy to buy. Hard to optimize.

cards.title: Thomas: “We pay a lot for a tool at 30%”
cards.description: Low adoption, empty CMDB, broken SLAs. Freshservice is deployed but underutilized. The board wants ROI — and you don’t have the numbers. Every month of inaction costs credibility.

cards.title: Mathieu: “Agentic AI isn’t a switch you flip”
cards.description: Freddy AI has impressive capabilities, but achieving 60-80% deflection requires a knowledge strategy, workflow design, and continuous measurement. Technology without methodology produces no results.

cards.title: CIO: “The last migration failed”
cards.description: 12+ years of accumulated customization on ServiceNow. Migrating to Freshworks without losing history or disrupting production — every successful migration saves €500K+, but the risk paralyzes the decision.

cards.title: Nadia: “Onboarding still runs on email”
cards.description: HR, legal, facilities — everything runs on shared files and emails. 75% of companies underutilize ESM. The potential to extend beyond IT is massive — and untapped.

testimonials.quote: Eric structured our CMDB, automated our workflows and trained our teams. In 4 weeks, adoption went from 30 to 85%.
testimonials.author: Sophie M.

testimonials.quote: Our CSAT went from 62 to 91% in two months. Freddy’s chatbot handles over half of L1 tickets. We finally have full visibility on our support.

about.headline: One point of contact, not a firm

team.name: Eric Sibomana
team.bio: 15 years of professional experience including 5 in consulting, 40+ transformation projects delivered. I support executive and operational leadership through complex transformations: strategic diagnostics, process redesign, change management, and program steering. Active reference: IT services transformation at Bonduelle Group (€2B revenue, 39 sites, 100 countries). Every engagement delivers measurable results in 4 weeks.

items.q: What does a Freshworks consultant do?
items.a: A Freshworks consultant is a certified expert who deploys, migrates, and optimizes Freshservice (ITSM) and Freshdesk (customer support). Unlike generalist integrators, they know ITIL best practices and product limits to deliver in 4 weeks, not 6 months.

items.q: How much does a Freshservice implementation cost?
items.a: A Freshservice implementation starts at €5,000 for a standard ITSM scope (portal, workflows, CMDB). Cost varies with the number of integrations (EntraID, M365, ERP) and ESM departments. A free diagnostic helps define the exact budget.

items.q: How to migrate from ServiceNow to Freshservice?
items.a: ServiceNow → Freshservice migration follows 4 steps: audit of current setup, data mapping (tickets, CMDB, workflows), incremental migration with validation, and training. Zero data loss, zero service interruption. Average duration: 6 to 8 weeks.

items.q: How long does a Freshdesk deployment take?
items.a: An omnichannel Freshdesk deployment (email, chat, phone, WhatsApp) takes 3 to 5 weeks. This includes SLA configuration, smart routing, bilingual knowledge base, and CRM integrations (Salesforce, HubSpot). Go-live is guaranteed.

items.q: Freshservice vs ServiceNow: what are the differences?
items.a: Freshservice costs 3–5× less than ServiceNow for mid-market scope (500–2,000 users). It deploys in weeks vs. months, without a dedicated admin team. However, ServiceNow remains relevant for enterprises (+10,000 users) with complex GRC needs.

items.q: How to improve ITSM tool adoption?
items.a: 70% of companies underuse their ITSM tool. Our 3-lever method: simplify the portal (reduce categories by 60%), automate repetitive tasks (Freddy AI), and train agents with concrete KPIs (CSAT, SLA). Measurable results in 4 weeks.

meta.title: Service Maturity Index™ — Assess Your Service Maturity
meta.description: Free assessment of your IT service, customer support and ESM maturity. 10 questions, instant results, personalized recommendations.

selector.headline: How mature are your services?
selector.subheadline: Choose the track that matches your challenge. Each assessment covers the key dimensions of your domain with specific recommendations.

itsm.title: IT Services (ITSM)
itsm.description: Assess your IT service maturity: incident management, change management, CMDB, automation, service catalog, and governance.

cx.title: Customer Support (CX)
cx.description: Assess your customer support maturity: ticket management, omnichannel, self-service, satisfaction, analytics, and proactive support.

esm.title: Employee Services (ESM)
esm.description: Assess your internal HR/Facility/IT services maturity: employee experience, requests & incidents, multi-department automation, governance, and analytics.

results.quickWinsTitle: Quick wins — your priority improvement areas
results.gateSubtitle: Score by dimension, industry benchmarks, and personalized recommendations — straight to your inbox.

heading.level1: Get back in control: where to start

demographics.title: A few details before we begin
demographics.subtitle: This helps us benchmark your results against similar organizations.

1a.text: How does your organization handle IT incidents and service requests?
1a.opt1: Incidents are reported via email, phone, or in person with no structured ticketing system. No consistent categorization or prioritization.
1a.opt2: We have a ticketing system, but categorization is inconsistent. Tickets are manually assigned and we don't always track response times.
1a.opt3: Tickets are auto-categorized with a priority matrix. SLAs are defined per priority level and we track compliance. Self-service handles common requests.
1a.opt4: AI-assisted categorization and routing. SLA compliance exceeds 95%. Trend analysis feeds problem management. CSAT measured per interaction.
1a.opt5: Predictive analytics identifies potential incidents before impact. Virtual agents autonomously resolve 40–60% of L1 requests.

1b.text: What is your approximate first-contact resolution (FCR) rate?
1b.opt1: We don't track FCR. Issues often require multiple contacts and escalations.
1b.opt2: FCR is estimated at 40–55%, but tracking is informal. Reopen rate is high (>10%).
1b.opt3: FCR is formally tracked at 65–75%. SLA compliance is 85–92%. Average MTTR is 4–8 business hours.
1b.opt4: FCR exceeds 80%. Reopen rate is under 5%. We track MTTR trends and benchmark regularly.
1b.opt5: FCR exceeds 85% with MTTR continuously declining. Self-service and AI deflection exceeds 50%. CSAT consistently > 90%.

2a.text: How does your organization manage IT changes and investigate recurring problems?
2a.opt1: Changes are made without formal approval. No CAB or change calendar. Recurring incidents are fixed each time they occur.
2a.opt2: We have a basic process, but not all changes go through it. Problem management is reactive.
2a.opt3: Change types (standard/normal/emergency) are defined. CAB meets regularly. RCA conducted for major incidents. Success rate 90–95%.
2a.opt4: Risk-based automated approval. Proactive problem management through trend analysis. Success rate > 97%.
2a.opt5: AI-assisted risk assessment integrated with CI/CD pipelines. Predictive impact analysis. Near-zero recurring incidents.

3a.text: How mature is your knowledge base and self-service portal?
3a.opt1: Knowledge lives in people's heads, scattered files, or sticky notes. No formal KB or portal.
3a.opt2: We have a KB, but it's sparsely populated and rarely updated. Portal exists but adoption is < 15%.
3a.opt3: Centralized KB covering 50–70% of common issues. Self-service adoption at 30–45%. Articles reviewed quarterly. Deflection 20–30%.
3a.opt4: KCS principles adopted. AI suggestions during ticket handling. Adoption 50–65%. Automated freshness alerts. Deflection 40–55%.
3a.opt5: AI generates article drafts. Contextual NLP search. Adoption > 65%. Deflection > 55%. KM program ROI measured.

4a.text: What level of automation exists in your IT service delivery?
4a.opt1: Nearly everything is manual — routing, approvals, assignments, and fulfillment all require human intervention.
4a.opt2: We have basic auto-routing rules and a few approval workflows, but most processes are manual.
4a.opt3: 30–40% of repetitive tasks are automated. Auto-assignment via load balancing. Workflow templates for common scenarios.
4a.opt4: AI-powered intelligent routing based on skills and context. Multi-system orchestration. 50–65% of standard requests auto-fulfilled.
4a.opt5: Agentic AI handles autonomous resolution for defined scenarios. Multi-system orchestration. 65–80% self-service automation.

5a.text: How does your organization track IT assets and configuration items?
5a.opt1: Assets are tracked in spreadsheets (if at all). No CMDB. Relationships between systems aren't documented.
5a.opt2: We have a basic inventory tool, but data is often outdated. No CI relationship mapping.
5a.opt3: CMDB implemented with key CI types. Agent-based discovery for hardware/software. CI relationships mapped. CMDB accuracy 70–85%.
5a.opt4: Fully automated discovery and reconciliation. Service maps linking business services to technical components. Accuracy > 90%.
5a.opt5: Real-time CMDB updates. Cloud assets auto-discovered. CMDB drives impact analysis, capacity planning, and cost optimization. Accuracy > 95%.

6a.text: How does your IT organization use data and analytics?
6a.opt1: No dashboards or formal reporting. Decisions are based on gut feeling or anecdotal evidence.
6a.opt2: We generate basic reports (ticket counts) compiled manually in spreadsheets.
6a.opt3: Standard dashboards tracking core KPIs (incident volume, SLA compliance, backlog). Monthly reporting. Trends analyzed.
6a.opt4: Real-time dashboards with automated alerts. AI assists in anomaly detection. Metrics correlated with business outcomes.
6a.opt5: Predictive and prescriptive analytics. AIOps integration. ML models for categorization and resolution prediction.

7a.text: How do employees request IT services?
7a.opt1: No service catalog. Employees email, call, or walk up. Available services aren't documented anywhere.
7a.opt2: We have a basic list of services, but forms are inconsistent. Fulfillment is mostly manual.
7a.opt3: Centralized catalog on a self-service portal with standardized forms. SLAs defined per category. 20–40% of standard requests automated.
7a.opt4: The catalog is the primary entry point. Over 60% of standard requests auto-fulfilled. Integrated with CMDB and HR.
7a.opt5: Enterprise Service Management: catalog extended beyond IT (HR, facilities, legal). AI recommendations. > 80% auto-fulfillment.

8a.text: How does your IT organization govern services and drive improvement?
8a.opt1: No formal governance structure. Improvements only happen after major incidents. IT is seen as a cost center.
8a.opt2: Some processes are documented, but no formal service owners or CSI program.
8a.opt3: Governance structure with defined roles. CSI register maintained. Quarterly service reviews. CSAT surveys after resolution.
8a.opt4: Data-driven governance. Regular benchmarking. Executive dashboards linking IT metrics to business outcomes.
8a.opt5: Continual improvement is in the DNA. Full financial management. IT is a strategic partner co-creating business value.

1.label: Firefighting
1.desc: Ad hoc, reactive, undocumented. Success depends on individual heroics.

2.label: Stabilizing
2.desc: Basic ticketing in place, repeatable processes but not standardized.

3.label: Established
3.desc: Organization-wide standards, defined processes, SLAs tracked.

4.label: Optimized
4.desc: Data-driven governance, quantitative control, proactive management, automation at scale.

5.label: Leading
5.desc: Continual improvement embedded, AI-driven operations, IT as strategic partner.

commercial.incident: Organizations at your maturity level typically spend 40% more on reactive incident management.
commercial.change: Your success rate suggests a loss of X hours/month on failed changes.
commercial.knowledge: Raising self-service from 15% to 45% typically deflects 2,000+ tickets/year.
commercial.automation: Every manual intervention costs €15–25. Automating password resets and access requests saves 200+ hours/year.
commercial.assets: Without accurate asset data, every change carries blind risk.
commercial.analytics: You're collecting data but not acting on it — a pattern seen in 60% of mid-market organizations.
commercial.catalog: A well-designed catalog reduces ticket volume by 20–30%.
commercial.governance: The gap between ITSM ambition and reality is the number one finding in our audits.

benchmark.incident: Average FCR: 70–75% | Target SLA: 90%+ | MTTR: 4–8 business hours
benchmark.knowledge: Self-service: €1–4/ticket vs. €17–25 phone | Mature KB = +37% CSAT
benchmark.automation: Level 5: +40–60% productivity gains | Freddy AI: 76.6% faster
benchmark.assets: 67% of SMBs still use spreadsheets for ITSM-ITAM integration
benchmark.analytics: 71% consider AI important, but adoption is limited to 34%
benchmark.catalog: Level 3 portal adoption: 40–60% | ESM = maturity marker
benchmark.governance: 56% see ITSM as strategic, but only 12% are mature

1a.text: How does your organization handle customer support tickets?
1a.opt1: Requests come in via email with no structured system. No response time tracking or SLAs.
1a.opt2: We have a basic ticketing system, but routing is manual. Response times are not consistently tracked.
1a.opt3: Tickets are auto-categorized with defined SLAs. SLA compliance tracked at 85-92%. First response time < 4h.
1a.opt4: AI assists triage and prioritization. SLA compliance > 95%. First response time < 1h. CSAT measured per interaction.
1a.opt5: Predictive resolution. Virtual agents resolve 40-60% of L1 requests. Median resolution time < 17h. CSAT > 90%.

1b.text: What is your first-contact resolution (FCR) rate?
1b.opt1: We don't measure FCR. Customers often have to contact us multiple times for the same issue.
1b.opt2: FCR is estimated at 50-60%, but tracking is imprecise. Reopen rate exceeds 10%.
1b.opt3: FCR is tracked at 70-75%. Reopen rate < 8%. Agents have access to full customer history.
1b.opt4: FCR exceeds 80%. AI suggests real-time solutions. Reopen rate < 4%.
1b.opt5: FCR exceeds 85%. Single-contact resolution for 84%+ of requests. ML-optimized routing.

2a.text: How many support channels do you offer and are they integrated?
2a.opt1: Primarily email. Other channels (social media, chat) get inconsistent responses.
2a.opt2: 2-3 channels (email + phone, maybe chat) but siloed. Customers have to repeat their issue.
2a.opt3: 3-4 channels (email, chat, phone, social) with shared CRM and unified customer view. Consistent SLAs.
2a.opt4: True omnichannel: context preserved across channels. Full history visible. AI routing by request type.
2a.opt5: Seamless channel switching mid-conversation. Proactive suggestions based on customer preferences. Unified analytics.

3a.text: How effective is your customer self-service ecosystem?
3a.opt1: A basic FAQ page or no self-service at all. Less than 10% of customers resolve issues on their own.
3a.opt2: A help center with a few articles, but search is weak and content often outdated. Deflection < 20%.
3a.opt3: Structured knowledge base with search. Basic chatbot. Customer portal. Deflection 30-50%.
3a.opt4: AI chatbot resolving 40-60% of queries. Active community forums. Predictive recommendations.
3a.opt5: Conversational AI resolving 60%+ of queries. Automatic content gap detection. Deflection > 60%.

4a.text: How equipped and productive are your support agents?
4a.opt1: No canned responses, no internal KB, no productivity tools. Training limited to onboarding.
4a.opt2: A few canned responses. Basic training. Manual ticket assignment.
4a.opt3: Response library. Internal KB. Skill-based auto-routing. Regular training. Performance dashboards.
4a.opt4: AI agent assist with real-time suggestions. Predictive workload distribution. Analytics-based coaching.
4a.opt5: AI handles L1, freeing agents for complex issues. Integrated career paths. Agent satisfaction measured and optimized.

5a.text: How do you measure and act on customer feedback?
5a.opt1: No formal satisfaction measurement. Feedback is anecdotal, relayed by sales reps.
5a.opt2: Occasional CSAT surveys with low response rate. No NPS or CES.
5a.opt3: Post-interaction CSAT. Quarterly NPS. Monthly feedback review. Negative feedback followed up within 1 week.
5a.opt4: Multi-metric approach (CSAT + NPS + CES). Automated closed-loop within 24h. CX metrics at executive level.
5a.opt5: Real-time sentiment analysis. Predictive satisfaction modeling. CX metrics drive product roadmap.

6a.text: How does your support use data and AI?
6a.opt1: No dashboards. Decisions are based on intuition or gut feeling.
6a.opt2: Basic reports (ticket volumes, average times) compiled manually.
6a.opt3: Standard dashboards tracking KPIs (volume, SLA, backlog, CSAT). Monthly reporting. Trends analyzed.
6a.opt4: Real-time dashboards with alerts. AI detects anomalies. Metrics correlated with business outcomes.
6a.opt5: Predictive and prescriptive analytics. ML models for routing and resolution prediction. AIOps integration.

7a.text: How do you manage knowledge for customer support?
7a.opt1: Knowledge lives in agents' heads. No documented knowledge base.
7a.opt2: A few basic articles, rarely updated. Agents ask colleagues for complex cases.
7a.opt3: Centralized KB with 50-70% coverage. Articles reviewed quarterly. Suggestions to agents during ticket handling.
7a.opt4: KCS principles adopted. AI suggests relevant articles. Stale content alerts. Coverage > 80%.
7a.opt5: AI generates article drafts from resolved tickets. Contextual NLP search. KM program ROI measured.

8a.text: How does your organization practice proactive support?
8a.opt1: 100% reactive support. We wait for the customer to report a problem.
8a.opt2: Occasional manual notifications for major incidents. No proactive monitoring.
8a.opt3: Automated alerts for known outages. Proactive communication on maintenance. Proactive FAQ on trending topics.
8a.opt4: Proactive monitoring of customer health indicators. Automated outreach for at-risk customers. Customer health scoring.
8a.opt5: Predictive support: contacting customers BEFORE they encounter a problem. AI identifies churn signals. Customer success integrated with support.

1.label: Reactive
1.desc: 100% reactive support, no structured processes. Success relies on individual effort.

2.label: Developing
2.desc: Basic ticketing in place, a few active channels but siloed.

3.label: Proactive
3.desc: Defined processes, emerging omnichannel, SLAs tracked, operational analytics.
3.cta: Learn how to reach Customer-centric level

4.label: Customer-centric
4.desc: Data-driven, integrated AI, seamless omnichannel experience, NPS-driven.

5.label: Champion
5.desc: Predictive support, autonomous AI, CX as a strategic competitive advantage.

commercial.tickets: Your resolution time puts you in the bottom quartile. Freshdesk auto-triage + canned responses can cut it by 50% in 90 days.
commercial.omnichannel: Customers who switch channels without preserved context are 4x more likely to churn.
commercial.selfservice: Every 10% increase in self-service saves approximately €25,000–40,000/year in agent costs.
commercial.agents: Agent turnover costs €10,000–25,000 per replacement. Mature organizations retain 10x better.
commercial.satisfaction: You measure CSAT but not NPS or CES — you see satisfaction but not loyalty. Companies measuring all 3 grow 2.5x faster.
commercial.analytics: You're collecting data but not acting on it — a pattern seen in 60% of support organizations.
commercial.knowledge: Raising self-service from 15% to 45% deflects 2,000+ tickets/year. Cost: €0.50 self-service vs. €17–25 phone.
commercial.proactive: Proactive support reduces inbound volume by 20-30% and increases NPS by 15-20 points.

benchmark.tickets: Best-in-class FRT: <4h | FCR benchmark: 70-75% | Median SaaS resolution: 82h
benchmark.omnichannel: Champions: 2.1 more channels | Chat CSAT: 87% | 72% of customers prefer self-service
benchmark.selfservice: Average tech deflection: 23% | Best-in-class: 40-60%+ | Cost: €0.50–2 vs. €17–25 phone
benchmark.agents: 77% of agents report increasing workload | 56% experiencing burnout | Champions: 2.4 more training days/year
benchmark.satisfaction: Average SaaS CSAT: 68% | B2B SaaS NPS: 31-41 | CES > 90% = exceptional
benchmark.analytics: 71% consider AI important | Adoption limited to 34% | Freshdesk Freddy: 45%+ AI deflection
benchmark.knowledge: KB coverage Level 3: 50-70% | KCS = +37% CSAT | Self-service adoption target: 50-65%
benchmark.proactive: Inbound volume reduction: 20-30% | NPS +15-20 points | Predictive support = competitive advantage

1a.text: How do your employees access internal services (HR, IT, Facility)?
1a.opt1: Email, phone or Slack on a case-by-case basis. Each team has its own channels. Employees often don't know who to reach.
1a.opt2: Separate portals per department, with inconsistent forms. Employees juggle multiple tools depending on their request.
1a.opt3: Single portal with a service catalog structured by department. Requests are categorized and tracked end-to-end.
1a.opt4: Single portal with personalization per employee profile. Federated search. Auto-suggested services based on context (mobility, onboarding).
1a.opt5: Conversational experience (HR/IT/Facility chatbot). Service design driven by employee feedback. Internal NPS measured and correlated with retention.

1b.text: How well is your internal service catalog aligned to key employee moments (onboarding, mobility, parental leave, offboarding)?
1b.opt1: No formal catalog. HR/managers improvise per case. New hires discover processes as they go.
1b.opt2: A few process pages documented but scattered. Onboarding is partially tooled, the rest is manual.
1b.opt3: Catalog covering the main moments (onboarding, exit). Workflows defined for the most common requests. Explicit SLAs.
1b.opt4: Employee journeys tooled end-to-end (onboarding, mobility, parental leave). Automated coordination across HR × IT × Facility for each key moment.
1b.opt5: Continuous service design: every journey is measured (time, satisfaction, drop-off) and improved iteratively. Employee experience is a managed product.

2a.text: How are internal requests handled (access, equipment, leave, payroll, badge…)?
2a.opt1: Email tickets or shared spreadsheets. No systematic categorization. Tracking depends on operational team memory.
2a.opt2: Ticketing tool in place but partially used. No consistent SLAs. Lead times vary widely depending on the request.
2a.opt3: Structured ticketing with categories, priorities and SLAs. Manual but documented routing. Backlog tracked monthly.
2a.opt4: AI auto-routing based on category and skill. SLA compliance > 90%. 360° view of employee history. Cross-department coordination.
2a.opt5: Predictive resolution: AI anticipates demand peaks (back-to-school, fiscal periods). 40-60% of L1 requests resolved via self-service or bot.

2b.text: What is your average resolution time for a routine employee request (badge, access, simple HR request)?
2b.opt1: More than 5 working days. Employees follow up multiple times and don't know where their request stands.
2b.opt2: 2 to 5 days. Partial visibility for the employee. Escalations are informal.
2b.opt3: 1 to 2 days for standard requests. SLAs tracked and communicated. Status available in self-service.
2b.opt4: Less than 24h for 80% of routine requests. Automated notifications at every step. Systematic feedback loop.
2b.opt5: Instant resolution for most requests (automated provisioning, conversational bots). Lead times < 2h on requests requiring human action.

3a.text: How are cross-department processes (onboarding, mobility, exit) orchestrated?
3a.opt1: Each team (HR, IT, Facility) triggers its tasks manually. Many oversights, duplicates and tasks falling through the cracks.
3a.opt2: Shared checklists (Excel, Notion). Orchestration relies on emails and the vigilance of an HR coordinator.
3a.opt3: Automated workflows for onboarding and offboarding. HR/IT/Facility tasks triggered in cascade from a source event (start date).
3a.opt4: Multi-department orchestration driven by a workflow engine. Exception handling, automatic escalations, conductor view for HR.
3a.opt5: Event- and data-driven orchestration: role changes, return from parental leave, end of mission automatically trigger the right workflows. Full audit trail.

3b.text: What is the integration level between your HRIS, your ITSM and your Facility tools?
3b.opt1: No integration. Employee data is entered manually in each tool. Risk of drift between systems.
3b.opt2: Occasional file imports (CSV, batch). Several days of lag between systems.
3b.opt3: API or connectors between HRIS and ITSM. Daily sync of active employees. Facility tools still in silo.
3b.opt4: Real-time integration HRIS ↔ ITSM ↔ Facility. Automatic provisioning of access, badges and equipment on arrival.
3b.opt5: Event-driven architecture: any HR change (role, team, status) automatically propagates to rights, equipment, spaces. Unified employee reference data.

4a.text: How do you manage compliance (GDPR, collective agreements, traceability) across your employee services?
4a.opt1: No formal mapping of employee data. Compliance relies on HR/IT teams' goodwill.
4a.opt2: A few hand-maintained registers. Employee data access requests (GDPR) are handled case by case, sometimes off-deadline.
4a.opt3: Up-to-date processing register. Formal workflows for employee rights (access, deletion). GDPR deadlines met.
4a.opt4: Compliance by design: retention periods, anonymization and audit trail driven from the platform. Automated reports to DPO and works councils.
4a.opt5: Continuous employee data governance: automated DPIAs on each new use case, permanent controls, integration with the global security policy.

5a.text: How do you measure employee satisfaction and experience on internal services?
5a.opt1: No formal measurement. Sentiment surfaces via word of mouth or annual reviews.
5a.opt2: Annual engagement survey, with no specific measurement on internal services. Low response rate.
5a.opt3: Post-resolution CSAT on HR/IT tickets. Quarterly pulse. Monthly feedback review by service leads.
5a.opt4: ENPS (Employee NPS) on internal services measured continuously. Closed feedback loop < 7 days on negative responses. Indicators reported to the HR committee.
5a.opt5: Sentiment analysis on tickets and conversations. Predictive models linking service experience × engagement × retention. ENPS published and managed.

5b.text: How do you leverage ESM data to steer and improve your internal services?
5b.opt1: No dashboards. Volumes are only known when leadership requests ad hoc reporting.
5b.opt2: Monthly manual reporting (volumes, lead times). No cross-referencing with other sources (HRIS, payroll).
5b.opt3: Standard dashboards: volumes, SLAs, top categories, resolution time. Quarterly reporting to HR/Operations leadership.
5b.opt4: Real-time dashboards with alerts. Cross-referencing ESM × HRIS × engagement. Automatic anomaly detection (request peaks, SLA drift).
5b.opt5: Predictive analytics: anticipation of peaks (back-to-school, payroll, year-end), dynamic resource allocation, ROI of internal services measured and published.

1.label: Firefighting
1.desc: No ESM catalog, requests handled case by case, little visibility. Employees reach informal contacts and HR absorbs escalations.

2.label: Reactive
2.desc: Tools in place but siloed by department. Processes are partially documented, SLAs drift and cross-team coordination remains manual.

3.label: Managed
3.desc: Unified portal, structured ESM catalog, SLAs tracked. Key cross-department processes (onboarding, exit) are automated.
3.cta: See how to reach Strategic level

4.label: Strategic
4.desc: Data-driven service design, automated multi-department orchestration, ENPS tracked continuously. Employee experience is an employer brand lever.

5.label: Optimized
5.desc: Predictive, event-driven ESM. Internal services are managed as a product, measured on engagement and retention. ROI published to the leadership committee.

commercial.experience: An average employee experience score correlates with +25% turnover. Industrializing service design typically reduces replacement cost by €10,000–25,000 per role.
commercial.incident: Each manually handled HR/IT request costs €15–30. Automating 40% of routine requests saves €150–250k/year for 1,000 employees.
commercial.automation: An unorchestrated onboarding loses 1 to 2 productive days per new hire. For 100 hires/year, that's €200k of lost productivity.
commercial.governance: A major GDPR non-compliance exposes you to 4% of global revenue. A maintained, auditable ESM register reduces risk and accelerates CNIL/works council audits.
commercial.analytics: Organizations that measure ENPS and link it to internal services show engagement up by 17 points and retention improved by 21%.

benchmark.experience: Median B2B ENPS: 0–20 | Best-in-class: 40+ | Service design cuts onboarding lead time by 30–50%
benchmark.incident: Manual HR/IT ticket cost: €15–30 | Mature self-service: €1–4 | ESM deflection target: 30–45%
benchmark.automation: Tooled onboarding: −40% HR × IT time | Automatic provisioning: D0 vs. typical D+5 | Multi-department workflows: −60% data re-entry
benchmark.governance: Legal GDPR deadline: 1 month | Best-in-class: < 7 days | Automated DPIA: −70% DPO effort
benchmark.analytics: Continuous ENPS adoption: 24% | Champions: ENPS published to executive committee | Peak prediction accuracy: 80–90%

meta.title: ITSM Maturity Level 1 — Firefighting Mode
meta.description: Your ITSM is in firefighting mode. Discover priority actions to structure your IT services with Freshservice.

level-1.headline: Firefighting mode: your IT services react instead of lead
level-1.intro: An ITSM maturity score below 20/100 indicates reactive, unstructured IT processes. Incidents are handled case by case, without categorization or prioritization. There is no self-service portal or CMDB. On average, 70% of companies at this level waste 15 hours/week on manual triage.
problems: No self-service portal — 100% of requests come through email or phone
No categorization or SLAs — impossible to measure performance
No CMDB — assets are not tracked
No automation — every ticket is processed manually
recommendations: Deploy a Freshservice portal with a basic service catalog (5-10 categories)
Configure SLAs by priority (P1: 1h, P2: 4h, P3: 8h, P4: 24h)
Launch Discovery to automatically inventory assets
Train agents on triage and routing (2 days)
level-1.cta: Get a free diagnostic of your ITSM

meta.title: CX Maturity Level 1 — Reactive Support
meta.description: Your customer support is reactive and disorganized. Discover how to structure Freshdesk to improve CSAT.

level-1.headline: Reactive support: your customers wait too long
level-1.intro: A CX score below 20/100 means your customer support operates without structure. Emails pile up, SLAs don't exist, and CSAT isn't measured. On average, companies at this level have a first response time over 24h and CSAT below 50%.
problems: Single channel (email) — no chat, phone, or self-service
No SLAs configured — response times are not tracked
No knowledge base — agents repeat the same answers
CSAT not measured — no visibility on customer satisfaction
recommendations: Deploy Freshdesk with 3 channels: email + chat + web form
Configure SLAs by request type (urgent, standard, info)
Create a knowledge base with the top 20 most frequent questions
level-1.cta: Get a free diagnostic of your customer support
